class_schedule.topic: Communication Across Borders: Frameworks and Challenges
learning_objectives: Define international communication and its disciplinary foundations
Analyze how media systems shape information flows across borders
Evaluate the concept of information sovereignty
readings: McPhail, Global Communication: Theories, Stakeholders, and Trends Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: International communication examines how information, ideas, and narratives flow across borders and how this shapes international relations, culture, and power.
key_concepts: International communication as a field: media studies meets IR
Media systems: liberal, democratic corporatist, polarized pluralist (Hallin & Mancini)
Information flows: the debate over cultural imperialism vs. cultural hybridity
News agencies and global information infrastructure
Digital transformation of international communication
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative media analysis: how the same international event is covered in five different countries' media
setup_requirements: News articles from 5 countries on the same event

review.value: The theory that dominant countries (especially the US) impose their cultural products and values on weaker countries through media exports, undermining local cultures
Hallin and Mancini's comparative framework categorizing media systems by political parallelism, professionalization, state intervention, and market orientation

class_schedule.topic: Propaganda, Public Diplomacy, and Strategic Communication
learning_objectives: Distinguish between propaganda, public diplomacy, and strategic communication
Analyze historical and contemporary examples of state-sponsored communication
Evaluate the effectiveness of public diplomacy programs
readings: Cull, Public Diplomacy: Foundations for Global Engagement Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: States have always used communication as an instrument of power. The distinction between informing, persuading, and manipulating is at the heart of international communication.
key_concepts: Propaganda: definition, techniques, and historical examples (WWI, WWII, Cold War)
Public diplomacy: listening, advocacy, cultural diplomacy, exchanges
Strategic communication: integrated messaging to achieve policy objectives
Nation branding: countries as brands competing for tourists, investors, talent
The credibility challenge: why soft power requires authenticity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a public diplomacy campaign: evaluating the BBC World Service or Al Jazeera's global strategy

review.value: Government-sponsored programs to engage foreign publics through information, cultural exchanges, and dialogue, aiming to build understanding and influence rather than coerce
The strategic effort to manage a country's reputation and image internationally, integrating tourism, trade, investment, and cultural promotion into a coherent brand

class_schedule.topic: Social Media, Disinformation, and Information Warfare
learning_objectives: Analyze how social media has transformed international communication
Evaluate state-sponsored disinformation campaigns and their impact
Assess responses to disinformation: regulation, media literacy, platform accountability
readings: Wardle & Derakhshan, Information Disorder (Council of Europe report excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media has democratized international communication but also weaponized it. Disinformation campaigns now threaten democratic processes and international stability.
key_concepts: Social media as a tool of international communication: reach, speed, virality
Disinformation vs. misinformation vs. malinformation
Russian information warfare: IRA, RT, Sputnik, and the 2016 US election
Chinese information operations: Wolf Warrior diplomacy, TikTok, and AI-generated content
Responses: EU Code of Practice, platform content moderation, media literacy education
lecture_notes.demonstration: Disinformation detection exercise: identifying and analyzing a real disinformation campaign

review.value: Wardle and Derakhshan's framework distinguishing mis-information (false, no harm intent), dis-information (false, intentional harm), and mal-information (true, used to harm)
The strategic use of information and communication tools to undermine adversaries' decision-making, social cohesion, and public trust without conventional military force

class_schedule.topic: Media Freedom, Censorship, and Digital Authoritarianism
learning_objectives: Analyze global trends in media freedom and press safety
Evaluate digital authoritarianism: internet shutdowns, surveillance, content control
Assess international frameworks for protecting media freedom
readings: Reporters Without Borders, World Press Freedom Index (latest)
Feldstein, The Rise of Digital Repression Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Press freedom is declining globally while digital tools give authoritarian governments unprecedented capacity to control information and surveil citizens.
key_concepts: Global press freedom trends: decline for over a decade
Journalist safety: killings, imprisonment, legal harassment
Digital authoritarianism: Great Firewall, internet shutdowns, surveillance exports
Splinternet: the fragmentation of the global internet along political lines
International protection mechanisms: UNESCO, OSCE, and their limitations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Press freedom mapping: comparing media environments across regime types using RSF data

review.value: The use of digital technology by authoritarian governments to surveil, censor, and control citizens, including internet shutdowns, content filtering, social media monitoring, and surveillance technology exports
The fragmentation of the global internet into distinct, nationally controlled networks, exemplified by China's Great Firewall and Russia's sovereign internet law

class_schedule.topic: Global Media Industries and Cultural Flows
learning_objectives: Analyze the structure of global media industries: ownership, concentration, and power
Evaluate the flow of cultural products: Hollywood, Bollywood, K-wave, Nollywood
Assess debates about media diversity and cultural policy

lecture_notes.introduction: Global media industries shape what the world watches, reads, and believes. Understanding their structure and the flows of cultural products is essential for international communication.
key_concepts: Media conglomerates: ownership concentration and global reach
Contra-flow: non-Western media challenging Hollywood dominance
Korean Wave (Hallyu): K-pop, K-drama as soft power
Nollywood and Bollywood: alternative cultural production centers
Cultural policy: quotas, subsidies, and the UNESCO Convention on Cultural Diversity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Media flow analysis: mapping the global distribution of a streaming platform's content by country of origin
setup_requirements: Streaming content data by origin country

review.value: The flow of media content from the Global South and non-Western countries to the West and globally, challenging the one-way cultural imperialism thesis -- exemplified by K-pop, Bollywood, Turkish telenovelas
The 2005 convention affirming states' right to adopt cultural policies that protect and promote diversity of cultural expressions, including media quotas and subsidies for local content

class_schedule.topic: AI, Deepfakes, and the Future of International Communication
learning_objectives: Analyze the impact of AI on international communication: content generation, targeting, manipulation
Evaluate the deepfake threat to trust and verification
Assess governance frameworks for AI in the information ecosystem
readings: Chesney & Citron, 'Deep Fakes: A Looming Challenge for Privacy, Democracy, and National Security'
UNESCO, Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Artificial intelligence is the next revolution in international communication. Its potential for both empowerment and manipulation is unprecedented.
key_concepts: AI-generated content: text, image, video, audio
Deepfakes: political manipulation, fraud, and erosion of trust
Liar's dividend: how deepfakes allow real events to be dismissed as fake
AI in journalism: automated reporting, verification tools, language translation
Governance: EU AI Act, international norms for AI in information
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deepfake detection workshop: identifying AI-generated content using available tools

review.value: The phenomenon where the existence of deepfakes allows anyone to dismiss genuine evidence as fabricated, undermining accountability and truth in public discourse
The European Union's comprehensive AI regulation categorizing AI systems by risk level and imposing requirements including transparency, human oversight, and prohibition of certain uses

questions.question: Distinguish between disinformation, misinformation, and malinformation using Wardle and Derakhshan's framework. Provide one example of each in an international context.
questions.answer: Misinformation is false content shared without harmful intent (e.g., a tourist sharing an inaccurate historical claim about a foreign country on social media). Disinformation is deliberately false content created to cause harm (e.g., Russia's Internet Research Agency creating fake social media accounts to spread false narratives about NATO during the 2016 US election). Malinformation is genuine information shared with harmful intent (e.g., leaking authentic diplomatic cables to embarrass a government and damage international relationships, as in the WikiLeaks releases). The framework is useful because it distinguishes the intent and truthfulness dimensions, helping craft appropriate responses.

rubric.key: Shows understanding of the framework's utility

questions.question: What is public diplomacy and how does it differ from propaganda? Why does credibility matter more for public diplomacy than for propaganda?
questions.answer: Public diplomacy involves government-sponsored programs to engage foreign publics through information, cultural exchanges, dialogue, and advocacy, aiming to build understanding and positive relationships. Propaganda is the systematic dissemination of information to promote a particular cause, often using deception, emotional manipulation, and simplification. The key difference is that public diplomacy aspires to a two-way dialogue based on truthful information, while propaganda is one-way and may be deceptive. Credibility matters more for public diplomacy because its effectiveness depends on long-term trust: if a country's public diplomacy is perceived as propaganda, it loses credibility and becomes counterproductive. Propaganda can achieve short-term effects through emotional manipulation even without credibility, but public diplomacy aims for sustained influence that requires audiences to trust the source.

rubric.key: Explains why credibility matters more for PD

quizzes.topic: Media Freedom, Digital Authoritarianism, and Cultural Flows

questions.question: What is the 'splinternet' and what are its implications for international communication? Identify two forces driving internet fragmentation.
questions.answer: The splinternet refers to the fragmentation of the global internet into distinct, nationally or regionally controlled networks with different rules, content, and access. Implications include: reduced access to information across borders, difficulty for international communication and collaboration, divergent technology standards, and the inability of global platforms to operate uniformly. Two driving forces: (1) Authoritarian control -- China's Great Firewall blocks foreign platforms and content, creating a self-contained Chinese internet with domestic alternatives (WeChat, Weibo, Baidu); Russia's sovereign internet law enables disconnection from the global internet. (2) Data sovereignty and privacy regulation -- the EU's GDPR, data localization requirements in India and Russia, and national security concerns about foreign platforms all create distinct regulatory environments that fragment the internet along jurisdictional lines, even among democratic states.

questions.question: What is the 'contra-flow' thesis in international communication and what evidence supports it? Does contra-flow disprove the cultural imperialism thesis?
questions.answer: The contra-flow thesis argues that cultural and media products increasingly flow from non-Western countries to the West and globally, challenging the one-directional model of cultural imperialism. Evidence includes: the global success of K-pop and Korean entertainment (BTS, Squid Game); Bollywood's massive global audience; Turkish and Latin American telenovelas exported worldwide; Nollywood producing more films annually than Hollywood; and Al Jazeera providing an alternative to Western news framing. However, contra-flow does not fully disprove cultural imperialism because: Hollywood and Western media still dominate global market share and revenue; global media infrastructure (platforms, distribution networks) remains largely Western-owned; and non-Western content often adapts to Western formats and tastes to achieve global success. A more accurate picture is cultural hybridity: complex, multi-directional flows with persistent Western structural advantages.

rubric.key: Evaluates relationship to cultural imperialism nuancedly

major_assignments.title: International Communication Campaign Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a real international communication campaign (public diplomacy, disinformation, social movement, or media initiative) and produce a comprehensive analysis evaluating its strategy, execution, and impact.
objectives: Apply international communication theory to a real case

criteria: Balanced assessment of success and failure
Theory-informed analysis of a real communication campaign

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on communication theory, media systems, and current events

participation.description: Engagement in media analysis exercises and discussions

midterm_examination.description: Exam on communication frameworks, media systems, and disinformation

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. More than 3 unexcused absences lower participation grade.
course_policies.collaboration: Media analysis exercises collaborative. Written analysis individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original with proper citation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to media databases, fact-checking tools, and social media analysis platforms.
